editionStmt.edition: Digital Critical Edition of the Chapter Summaries (Capitula) to Iudices in the Tours Bibles

publicationStmt.p:
Welcome to the digital critical edition project of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles!
The editions are part of the PRIN 2022 PNRR project DOBiPS – Data Oriented Biblical Paratext Studies , awarded for the 2023–2025 period to the research units at the Universities of Udine (P.I. E. Colombi) and Cassino (P.I. R. Casavecchia), under the competitive PRIN PNRR call – National Recovery and Resilience Plan, Mission 4 "Education and Research", funded by the European Union’s NextGenerationEU programme (protocol no. P2022ZW4AW).
The Capitula prefixed to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles have been encoded in XML/TEI and published using the software EVT (Edition Visualization Technology).
The editions were prepared by members of the Udine research unit: Emanuela Colombi, Margherita Filippozzi, and Silverio Franzoni.
Details of the encoding choices and related methodological reflections have been presented in the working paper: E. Colombi, M. Filippozzi, S. Franzoni, The Digital Critical Edition of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles , DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.16761173 .
Information on the individual editions is available by clicking the 'info' button at the top of the left-hand pane.

bibl.author: Donatien de Bruyne
bibl.title: Sommaires, divisions et rubriques de la Bible latine

bibl.author: Bonifatius Fischer
bibl.title: Lateinische Bibelhandschriften im frühen Mittelalter

bibl.author: Bernhard Bischoff
bibl.title: Katalog der festländischen Handschriften des neunten Jahrhunderts (mit Ausnahme der wisigotischen)

bibl.author: Robertus Weber
Roger Gryson
bibl.title: Biblia sacra iuxta Vulgatam versionem

bibl.author: Ulysse Robert
bibl.title: Heptateuchi. Versio latina antiquissima e codice Lugdunensi. Version latine du Deutéronome, de Josué et des Juges antérieure a Saint Jérôme, publiée d'après le manuscrit de Lyon

I + 382 + I folios
height 495 mm width 350 mm

source.p: Fifth period (853-c.900) of the Turonian production according to Fischer 's classification (p. 268).

listWit.witness:
text of the A series as printed by D. de Bruyne , p. 46.
digital facsimile

tagsDecl.rendition: the digital reproduction is b/w, impossible to determine color

handNotes.handNote: uncertainty whether in the scribe’s hand or added later

front.p:
This digital edition presents the reconstruction of the capitula of Series A (according to Donatien de Bruyne's classification) that precede the Book of Iudices, in the textual form known from the Tours scriptorium.
To enhance the clarity of the critical apparatus, certain graphical and commentary devices have been adopted:
– In the reconstructed text, readings characteristic of the Turonian textual form are highlighted in comparison with those of the Maurdramnus' Bible (Amiens, Bibliothèques d'Amiens Métropole, Ms. 7, Corbie, 772-781 = Am2) and the Tours Octateuch (Tours, Bibliothèque municipale, Ms. 10, Tours, 790-800 = Tu).
Bold text indicates adiaphoric readings, i.e. readings equivalent to those attested in Am2 and/ or Tu; whenever the Turonian corpus (= Φ) preserves the better reading over the inferior one found in Am2/ Tu, that reading is also highlighted in bold.
Italicised text signals readings that are clearly erroneous, either exclusive to the Tours Bibles (to the entire corpus or at least to the majority of the Turonian witnesses) or shared with Am2 and/ or Tu; these readings have been included in the main text, as the aim is to reconstruct the archetypal form of the chapter headings from which the Tours Bibles descend.
Lemmas in the main text, which are highlighted in various colors depending on the cause of the variation, flag readings that differ, showing either a divergence between the Turonian corpus (= Φ) and one or both of the other branches (Am2, Tu), or a divergence from the reading chosen by D. de Bruyne in his edition of the biblical summaries.
- Additionally, in the left window displaying the reconstructed text, clickable red dots provide brief annotations indicating whether a reading is adiaphoric (adiaph.) or erroneous (error), and whether it is attested only in the Tours Bibles (= Φ) or also in Am2 or Tu. The opposing witness is introduced with the abbreviation "vs".
Innovations of Am2 or Tu that are not shared with the Tours Bibles are not specifically marked, but can be identified through the apparatus displayed in the right-hand window.
– The Critical Apparatus includes notes, accessible via the "Critical note" tab when present, which comment where necessary on remarkable textual choices and the possible genesis of certain variants; for this purpose, a careful examination has been conducted not only of the Vulgate text, but also of the Septuagint and the Old Latin text of Iudices, as attested in the Codex Lugdunensis (Lyon, Bibliothèque municipale, 403, 6th cent. = VL 100, edited by U. Robert, Paris, 1900).
- Furthermore, brief annotations on the paleographic features of the witnesses are included as "witDetail" element, accessible via the XML tab in the Critical Apparatus, where they are deemed useful to clarify the codification.
- Finally, the "Analogues" tab - located near the "Critical Apparatus" tab in the top-right pane - lists the biblical passages (quoted from the Vulgate) to which each chapter heading refers based on the marginal numbering in the manuscripts - with the positioning of these numerals in Am1, Tu, and Sg1 serving as the primary reference. These passages are also accessible by clicking the small icon preceding each heading's numeral in the reconstructed text on the left. Additional commentary on the textual relationship between the headings and the biblical text, or on peculiar lexical choices, is provided where necessary and accessible via the "Bibliographic Reference" tab within the Analogues' apparatus.
These features allow users to quickly grasp the most salient textual phenomena and their philological implications.
This series of capitula appears to have been modelled on the Vulgate translation of Iudices. The evidence comes from lexical choices - specific words that match the Vulgate in comparison with the Old Latin version of the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) (e.g., ch. 7 aestiuo cenaculo : VL 100 superiori aestiuali; ch. 9 clauum : VL 100 palum; ch. 17 adulescens : VL 100 puer) - and from entire sentences that recall the exact phrasing of the Vulgate (e.g., ch. 3 malum in conspectu domini : VL 100 malignum ante dominum; ch. 5 hae sunt gentes quas dominus dereliquit : VL 100 et hae gentes quas reliquit iesus). The sole exception is in ch. 4, where the verb excitantur seems to echo the Old Latin reading (VL 100 et excitauit dominus), rather than the Vulgate's suscitauitque dominus.
Regarding the textual transmission, it is possible, firstly, to conjecture a common archetype prior to the Tours Octateuch, the Maurdramnus' Bible, and the entire Turonian corpus. This archetype would be responsible for certain alterations affecting the whole manuscript tradition: the erroneous numeral XXX in ch. 7, the meaningless transposition of et barac in ch. 8, and the deterior reading his in ch. 10. Moreover, the flourish of variants of ipsos spreuerunt in ch. 4, where the exact reading is preserved in the Tours Octateuch and in two Turonian Bibles (P1, K) can be traced back to a common exemplar in which the abbreviation mark was ambiguously positioned over two letters. Similarly, the discordance among witnesses regarding the orthography of adoliscente in ch. 17 may stem from a common exemplar in which both alternating variants (ad*le-/ ad*li-) were preserved.
In examining the relationship between the three branches of the manuscript tradition, it should first be noted that the Tours Octateuch attests to a different textual tradition, which involves the headings from ch. 11 to ch. 18 (except ch. 13 and 15). In comparison with the series transmitted by the Maurdramnus' Bible and the almost entire Turonian corpus, Tu’s headings provide a more precise reference to the biblical narration, adding details that are omitted in the standard series. In the current state of research, Tu’s variants have no other attestations, and their dependence on other series of capitula edited by D. de Bruyne can be ruled out. Their wording and phrasing are not so distinctly marked that the biblical text on which they were modelled can be determined with certainty, although ch. 12, 16, and 18 seem to suggest the Vulgate as their source (see the critical notes ad loc.).
On this basis, a direct dependence of Φ on the Tours Octateuch can be ruled out, with the exception of Troyes, Médiathèque Jacques-Chirac, Ms. 29 (Tr), which, on the contrary, follows the peculiar tradition of Tu - not only in ch. 11–18, but also in some unique adiaphoric readings that characterise Tu in the previous headings (e.g., the transposition in ch. 2, the omission of et in ch. 7, the Old Latin orthography of the Jewish name sisarrae in ch. 9, lambierunt instead of -erant in ch. 10). Tr follows Tu’s new series strictly in ch. 14 and 16–18, only partially in ch. 11; in ch. 12, the standard heading is written over an erasure, a fact that suggests an adherence to Tu’s series in the inferior layer of writing (see the critical notes in the apparatus).
The position of the Maurdramnus' Bible in the manuscript tradition stands out as quite ambiguous, as it shares innovations with both Tu (the altered Jewish names inahel/ inhael in ch. 9 and iebethe in ch. 13, and the inferior reading micha instead of micham in ch. 17) and Φ (iar in ch. 12, where the sole Z within the Turonian corpus has the better form iair, and the inferior reading revocito in ch. 17, where - however - no comparison with Tu is possible). Am2 also shows some unique errors (ch. 5, 8, 15), but these are not relevant, as they could be easily emended.
Within Φ, there are no shared innovations that define sub-groups; rather, the Turonian Bibles appear as independent apographs of a common exemplar. The unique innovations of each witness are insignificant, consisting mainly of slips and orthographical variations.

bibl.title: Idc 1:1 Post mortem iosue [...] et erit dux belli

item.title:
Iudas eligitur elegitur
dux iudex
belli

bibl.title: Idc 2:1 Ascenditque angelus domini de galgal

item.title:
Increpat populum dominus dominus populum
Φ praeter Tr + Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu pro excessu idolorum

bibl.title: Idc 2:8 Mortuus est autem iosue
bibl.note: . Cfr. Idc 2:11 feceruntque filii israhel malum in conspectu domini; 2:14 iratusque dominus contra israhel tradidit eos in manibus diripientium. The marginal numeral is omitted in the Maurdramnus' Bible.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.

item.title:
Post obitum iosue iosuae fecerunt israhelitae israhelite israhelitæ malum in conspectu domini quos qos quos
tradidit inimicis suis in praedam praed?m praedam prȩdam

bibl.title: Idc 2:16 Suscitauitque dominus iudices : VL100 et excitauit dominus iudices
bibl.note: . The opening of the chapter heading apparently conserves the Old Latin version, as attested in the Codex Lugdunensis, while the remainder follows the Vulgate text quite closely (cf. Idc 2:21-22 et ego non delebo gentes [...] ut in ipsis experiar israhel : VL100 et ego non adponam auferre virum [...] ad temptandum in eo israhel).

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.

item.title:
Excitantur a domino iudices ad a liberationem israhel etiam ipso ipsos
Φ praeter Tr Bm P1 K + Am2 (error) vs Tu spuenerunt Written with the abbreviation spūer- spuenerant spūerant spreuerunt sp̄uerunt ?preuerunt spreuerunt spuenerant spuenerunt praeuenerunt Written with the abbreviation p̄uenerunt spreueruut The correct reading is preserved in the Tours Octateuch and a few Turonian Bibles (L2 p.c., P1, K); indeed, the chapter heading refers to Idc 2:16 sed nec illos audire voluerunt in the Vulgate. The more widely attested reading within the Turonian corpus, and also that found in the Maurdramnus' Bible and Cod. Sang. 75 - may have resulted from a misplacement of the abbreviation mark (perhaps placed over -p and -u in the common exemplar), from sp̄uer- to spūer- (see the reading of the Alcuin's Bible [Bm]). While this explains how the error could have arisen, the intended meaning of the abbreviation mark in the manuscript witnesses, however, remains uncertain.
Φ praeter Sg1 L2 Bm P1 K (error) vs Am2 vs Tu quibus reseruauit gentes ut in ipsis experiatur expiriantur experiantur experiatur israhel

bibl.title: Idc 2:23 Dimisit ergo dominus omnes has nationes
bibl.note: . The marginal numeral appears at this point in the manuscripts. Cfr. Idc 3:1 Hae sunt gentes quas dominus dereliquit.

item.title: Hae haec haec hae The feminine plural nominative haec is a later form that supplanted the classical hae to become standard in Medieval Latin (cf. ThLL, 2700, s.v. hic, haec, hoc). sunt gentes quas dominus dereliquit dereliquid derelin dereliquit Sg1 was perhaps about to write "derelin[quit]", but stopped and corrected it. reliquit dereliquit

bibl.title: Idc 3:5 Itaque filii israhel habitaverunt

item.title:
Pro excessu idolorum idorum captiuati sunt a rege cusan Apparently corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile. chusan
Φ praeter Tr K + Am2 (adiaph.) vs Tu mesopotamiae mesopotamiæ mesopotamiȩ

bibl.title: Idc 3:12 Addiderunt autem filii israhel facere malum

item.title:
Item pro malis traduntur eglon regi moab quem interfecit ahoth ahot in cenaculo caenaculo cenabulo suo cum arte interfectis etiam decem X milibus moabitarum mohabitarum moabitharum et
Φ praeter Tr + Am2 (adiaph.) vs Tu quieuit terra XXX triginta octoginta As the numeral triginta is unsupported by any other evidence, it should be considered an error (perhaps due to the accidental loss of the first letter in LXXX) that originated in the common exemplar from which the three branches of the tradition stem. Both the Old Latin version as attested by the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) and the Vulgate read octoginta, aligning with the Septuagint (Idc 3:30 καὶ ἡσύχασεν ἡ γῆ ὀγδοήκοντα ἔτη).
annis

bibl.title: Idc 3:31 Post hunc fuit samgar

item.title: Samgar qui occidit sexcentos se centos sescentos uiros uomere The letter m- appears subpunctuated, though it is not clear if this mark is intentional.

bibl.title: Idc 4:1 Addideruntque filii israhel facere malum
bibl.note: . Cfr. Idc 4:21 Tulit itaque iahel uxor aber clavum [...] posuit supra tempus capitis eius clavum.

item.title:
Constantia mulieris mulieri iahel inahel inhael i·ahel Φ praeter Tr Z (recte) vs Am2 vs Tu quae quæ clauo clau clauo infixo timpore tempore tympore tempore timpore Φ praeter Tr (adiaph.) vs Am2 vs Tu sisarae sisare sisarrae sis??rȩ sisarȩ Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile. sisarȩ sisareae sisarȩ The variant of the Jewish name found in the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Turonian Bibles (with slight orthographical differences, and excepting Cod. Turon. 29) matches the form in the Greek text (e.g. Idc 4:2 ὁ ἄρχων τῆς δυνάμεως αὐτοῦ Σισαρα) and the Vulgate. In contrast, the variant in the Tours Octateuch (followed by Cod. Turon. 29) corresponds to the form found in the Old Latin version of the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100). sequitur canticum et barac barach bara
et barach et bara barac et The corruption in the word order is evident (cf. Idc 5:1 cecineruntque debbora et barac in the Vulgate) and must have originated in the common exemplar, since it is found in all three branches of the manuscript tradition. This misplacement occurred possibly because the syntagm et barac was added marginally in the archetype without clear marks to guide its proper insertion into the main text. The chapter heading should be restored as canticum debbore et barac, according to the biblical text, rather than canticum barac et debborae as D. de Bruyne did in his edition.
debbore debobore debbore debborae debore

bibl.title: Idc 6:1 Fecerunt autem filii israhel malum
bibl.note: . The marginal numeral appears at this point in the Tours Octateuch; in the Maurdramnus' Bible it is omitted, in Cod. Sang. 75 it is at Idc 6:8 haec dicit dominus deus israhel, as it does in two of the most ancient bibles within the Turonian corpus (Tr and P8). Cfr. Idc 6:11 Venit autem angelus domini et sedit.

item.title:
Post excessus excelsus populi consuetos consueto apparuit angelus domini gedeon degedeon ge?eon gedeon Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile. confortans ad proeliandum praeliandum subuertit subuetit etiam bahal baal bal bahal The variant of the Jewish name transmitted by the majority of the Turonian witnesses aligns with the Old Latin orthography of the Codex Lugdunensis(e.g. Idc 6:25 euerte aram bahal). In contrast, the remainder of the Turonian corpus, in accordance with the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Tours Octateuch, follows the Vulgate's form.
et optulit obtulit sacrificium domino ipsius uiri uri uiri trecenti CCC tricenti lambierant lambierunt Written with the abbreviation lambier̄ lambierunt lambierant The manuscript tradition attests unanimously the verb lambi-, which appears as a later form of lambere (cfr. ThLL, 898, s.v. lambo).
Φ praeter Tr + Am2 (adiaph.) vs Tu aquas ut canes his is A blank space appears between canes. and is with no visible traces of correction. The subject of excessit is Gedeon (Idc 8:27 fecitque ex eo gedeon in the Vulgate) , therefore a masculine pronoun is expected. The form his for the masculine nominative of is is attested (cfr. ThLL, 456, s.v. is, ea, id); nonetheless, this reading - which was apparently already present in the common exemplar, as the manuscript tradition is nearly unanimous - appears to be a deterior lectio. The better reading is was likely conjecturally restored in K.
postmodum excessit cum israhel isrl̄ israel

bibl.title: Idc 8:29 Abiit itaque hierobbaal filius ioas
bibl.note: . The marginal numeral is at this point in the manuscripts. The birth of Abimelech is mentioned shortly after, at Idc 8:31 genuit ei filium nomine abimelech.

app.witDetail: A later hand (XV saec.?) added an exegetical note about the death of Abimelech in the inner margin.

item.title:
De regno abimelech infirmo
infirmo The reading is written over an erasure.
Φ praeter Tr a.c. + Am2 vs Tu qui
occidit super unam petram septuaginta fratres suos et occidit super unam petram septuaginta fratres suos et
Since ch. 11 the Tours Octateuch represents a textual tradition that differs considerably from the series transmitted by the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Turonian corpus. In the current state of research, it has no other witnesses (except for Cod. Turon. 29, which apparently follows the Tours Octateuch for the summary of Judges). The source of this tradition is therefore unknown, as the wording of the heading mirrors exactly neither the Vulgate nor the Old Latin version as attested in the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100); a direct descent of the Tours Octateuch variants from other series edited by D. de Bruyne can be excluded. Here the adjective infirmo, which qualifies the kingship of Abimelech, is replaced by an explicit reference to Idc 9:5 et occidit fratres suos filios hierobbaal (VL 100: gedeon) septuaginta uiros super lapidem unum, which recounts how Abimelech obtained his power unjustly - that is to say, the cause that leads to God's curse and, subsequently, the fall and death of Abimelech. For the other divergences in this heading as transmitted by the Tours Octateuch, see the following critical notes.
moritur
ipse ipse
The addition of the pronoun ipse is necessary in the heading's syntactic structure of this heading as transmitted by the Tours Octateuch, as it serves to highlight Abimelech's death by contrasting it with the death of his seventy brothers mentioned previously.
de fragmento fragnento Written with the abbreviation fragn̄to flagmento petrae petre petrȩ petræ molae molæ molaris molaris molȩ mole molȩ The reading transmitted (with slight orthographic differences) by the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Turonian corpus closely echoes the biblical text: Idc 9:53 fragmen (VL 100: fragmentum) molae. However, the addition of petrae is redundant, as molae is itself a noun. In this sense, the reading of the Tours Octateuch, where it is replaced by the adjective molaris, represents a stylistic improvement.
Φ praeter Tr a.c. + Am2 (adiaph.) vs Tu
a muliere proiecto et de iudice thoa et iahir The erasure extends to the end of the line, but it is difficult to determine whether the space would have been sufficient for the variant of the heading as attested in Tu (s. below, ch. 12). In the final part of ch. 11, the Tours Octateuch adds specific details from the biblical narrative that are omitted in the summary found in the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Turonian corpus series: namely, that the millstone was dropped by a woman (Idc 9:53) and the kingship of Thola - erroneously written as thoa in the manuscript (Idc 10:1). Furthermore, the mention within this same chapter of the kingship of Iair, which the following chapter heading refers to in the standard series, necessitates the substitution of the entire ch. 12 (see below, and the critical note). Regarding Cod. Turon. 29, which apparently follows the peculiar readings of the Tours Octateuch in the summary of Judges, it is noteworthy that parts of this heading (specifically those where Tu differs from the standard series) and the entire following heading appear to have been written over an erasure (see the witDetail element by clicking the XML button in the apparatus). Consequently, although it is difficult to determine whether the erased space would have been sufficient for the variant chapter heading as attested in Tu, it seems very likely that the Tr scribe first wrote the heading from the Tours Octateuch and subsequently replaced it with the version from the standard Turonian series.

bibl.title: Idc 10:3 Huic successit iair galaadites

app.lem:
Regnum iar
air iair The form of this Jewish name is iair in both the Vulgate and the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100). This form was preserved - perhaps conjecturally restored - by Z alone among the Turonian Bibles.
app.rdg: item filii israhel peccatis ueteribus noua iungentes traditi sunt in manu phylisthiim et filiorum amon
app.witDetail: The chapter is written over an erasure, but it is difficult to determine whether the space would have been sufficient for the variant of the heading as attested in Tu (s. above, ch. 11).
app.note: As anticipated above, the kingship of Iair is mentioned in the previous heading according to the peculiar textual tradition of the Tours Octateuch. Consequently, the present heading has been modified to refer to Idc 10:6-7 instead of Idc 10:3 (nonetheless, the marginal rubricated numbering in Tu is still placed at 10:3, as it is in the Maurdramnus' Bible and Cod. Sang. 75). In contrast to the modification concerning ch. 11 (see the critical notes above), the wording in this instance closely echoes the Vulgate text: filii autem israhel peccatis ueteribus iungentes noua fecerunt malum in conspectu domini [...] tradidit eos in manu philisthim et filiorum ammon. In Cod. Turon. 29 the entire heading is written over an erasure and aligned with the end of the preceding one. It is difficult to determine whether the Tr scribe first wrote the peculiar variant of the Tours Octateuch (as was very probably done for the preceding heading: see the critical note at ch. 11) or wrote the standard version of this heading directly over the erasure, which may have contained only the Tu variant of ch. 11.

bibl.title: Idc 11:1 Fuit illo tempore iepthae galaadites

app.witDetail: The scribe accidentally skipped the whole chapter, but corrected the error by adding it in the blank space at the end of ch. 12 and 14.

app.note: Within the Turonian corpus, this Jewish name appears, with slight orthographic variants, as iepthe - a form consistent with the Vulgate and the Old Latin version (VL 100), Ιεφθαε in the Septuagint. I could not find any source for the reading found in the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Tours Octateuch (followed by Cod. Turon. 29).

app.witDetail: A later hand (XV saec.?) added a note providing nearly exactly the Vulgate text of Idc 11:40 to the heading.

bibl.title: Idc 12:8 Post hunc iudicavit israhel abessan

title.note: Φ praeter Tr a.c. + Am2 (adiaph.) vs Tu

app.rdg: qui fuit super israhel et post eum hailon et post hunc abdon
app.witDetail: A later hand (XV saec.?) added a note providing further references to the biblical text to the chapter heading.
Mistakenly transposed from the following chapter heading.
app.note: As seen in ch. 11, the distinctive heading of the Tours Octateuch includes references to biblical passages that are absent from the standard series of the Turonian corpus and the Maurdramnus' Bible. Specifically, it mentions the kingship of Ahialon - erroneously written as hailon - (Idc 12:11) and Abdon (Idc 12:13). While these same references appear in other summaries published by D. de Bruyne (e.g. series C, B, Is), a direct textual filiation of the Tours Octateuch heading from any of these other series can be ruled out. Cod. Turon. 29 follows the peculiar text of the Tours Octateuch, as it typically does in the summary of Judges; however, it apparently restores (albeit imperfectly) the standard reading iudex in place of Tu's de iudice.

bibl.title: Idc 13:2 Erat autem uir quidam [...] nomine manue.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.
A later hand added the proper numbering next to the chapter when correcting the numbering errors caused by the omission of chapter 13.

item.title:
Iudex manue manu
cuius cum cum fius cuius In Z two distinct layers of correction seem to be distinguishable: the first, by the main hand, which corrected - as it seems - cū fius to cuius, and the second, by a later hand (XV saec.?), which changed conjecturally the lemma to cui fuit. This is the same hand that restored the name of Manue's son - accidentally omitted - at the end of the heading, and added the marginal annotations to ch. 11, 13, and 14 (see above, and the witDetail element by clicking the XML button in the apparatus).
filius famosus samson
sampson

bibl.title: Idc 17:1 Fuit eo tempere vir quidam [...] nomine michas

app.rdg: de micha cuius mater de ducentis argenteis fecit sculptile filiumque suum idolo constituit sacerdotem
de micha cuius mater de ducentis argenteis fecit scultile filiumque suum idolo constituit sacerdotem
app.note: This heading, as transmitted by the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Turonian corpus (except Cod. Turon. 29), is quite generic, summarizing the story of Micah with the term excessus. On the contrary, the peculiar version of the heading found in the Tours Octateuch (followed by Cod. Turon. 29) provides more details and exact references to the biblical narrative: it mentions the idol that Micah's mother had made from two hundred pieces of silver (Idc 17:4) and Micah's son, whom he installed as a priest (Idc 17:5). The source of the Tours Octateuch tradition is difficult to detect, as it does not seem to be attested elsewhere. However, the term argenteis may indicate the Vulgate as the biblical version to which the heading refers, since it corresponds exactly to Jerome's translation (Idc 14:4 ducentos argenteos) of διακοσίους ἀργυρίου, while the Old Latin's CC argenti in Cod. Lugdunensis (VL 100) closely echoes the Greek construct. Also idolo echoes the Vulgate's fecit [...] uestem sacerdotalem et idola.

bibl.title: Idc 17:7 Fuit quoque alter adulescens

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is barely visible due to faded ink.

item.title:
De adoliscente aduliscente adulescente adulesicente The scribe may have read adulescente in the main text of his exemplar and the variant aduliscente added as an -i in the interline above. adoliscente adolescente adolescente The alternance between adolescens andadul- is well attested in the history of the Latin language since its earliest phases (cfr. ThLL, 794, s.v.). Regarding the variants ad*le- and ad*li-, the erroneous reading adulesicente in B2 (which may have stemmed from an exemplar containing both forms - perhaps the one with -e in the main text and the other with -i as a variant added in the interlinear space), together with the discordance between the three branches of the manuscript tradition and within the Turonian corpus, suggests that the common exemplar may have preserved both forms in some way.
leuita agnito quem cognoscentes uiri de tribu dan
apud ad apud micham micha micheam michæ
alienigena qui eum idolo suo fecerat sacerdotem tulerunt una cum ipso idolo alienigena qui eum idolo suo fecerat sacerdote tulerunt una cum ipso idolo
et
reuocito
reuocatio cito The reading likely originated from an exemplar with both the variants reuocato and -cito. reuocato reuocito reuocato The reading reuocito, which has no exact biblical correspondent, is attested almost unanimously in the manuscript tradition. While it is probably a deterior lectio, its appearance in written evidence is documented (cf. Oxford Latin Dictionary, s.v. voco).
a tribu ad tribu
dan
The scribe - perhaps misled by the layout of his exemplar - copied the last part of this heading inline at the end of the following one (s. below, ch. 18). in sacerdotio idoli perduxerunt
The scribe - likely misled by the layout of his exemplar - copied the last part of this heading inline at the end of the following one (s. below, ch. 18). As seen in the previous chapter headings, the peculiar tradition represented by the Tours Octateuch (and followed only by Cod. Turon. 29 within the Turonian corpus) contains more detailed references to the biblical text. However, both this series and the standard one found in the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Turonian corpus (except Tr) appear to be based on the Vulgate, as certain lexical choices suggest: Idc 18:3 et agnoscentes uocem adulescentis leuitae (but Tu's cognoscentes may have a precedent in Codex Lugdunensis [VL 100]: et ipsi cognouerunt uocem pueri leuitae); 18:18 tulerunt igitur qui intrauerant [...] idola; 18:27 tulerunt sacerdotem.
et de ciuitate lahis quam obtinuerunt in possessione The reference to the conquest of the city of Laish, which concludes the episode of Micah (Idc 18:27-31), is absent from the standard version of this heading. The phrasing of the Tours Octateuch tradition does not seem to provide significant clues for detecting its source.

bibl.title: Idc 19:1 Fuit quidam vir levites habitans
bibl.note: . The Maurdramnus' Bible mistakenly has the numeral 19 at this point, whereas the numeral 18 is found at Idc 20:22 rursum filii israhel et fortitudine et numero.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is barely visible due to faded ink.

app.lem:
cui contigit
contingit contigit contingit nefas
app.rdg: qui uxorem suam defunctam per scelus gabaonitarum in duodecim partibus frustra concidens singulas partes per duodecim tribus misit
app.note: While the heading in its standard form - found in the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Turonian corpus (except Cod. Turon. 29) - is very concise and vague, the version transmitted by the Tours Octateuch contains precise references to the biblical narrative. Its lexical choices and phrasing suggest the Vulgate as its source: it uses scelus (Idc 19:24, 20:3), instead of stultitiam and malitia of the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100); it quotes Idc 19:29 (in duodecim partes ac frusta [VL 100: membratim] concidens) almost verbatim, except for the corrupted frustra; and, although the Vulgate's terminos (Idc 19:29) is replaced by tribus, which aligns with the Old Latin version, the concept is readily recalled from the context (moreover, the same term tribus is also found in the Vulgate at Idc 20:2). The episode is also summarized in detail in other series edited by D. de Bruyne, but a textual relation with the Tours Octateuch's version can be excluded.
The Z scribe - perhaps misled by the layout of his exemplar - copied the last part of the previous heading inline at the end of this one (s. above, ch. 17).
